content.text: A model card published at deployment time and never updated is not a governance artifact — it is a historical document. AI systems change: models are retrained on new data, fine-tuned for improved performance, red-teamed and found to have previously undisclosed limitations, and incidents reveal failure modes that the initial card did not capture. The model card maintenance cadence defines how these changes trigger card updates, who reviews and approves the updates, how updates are version-controlled, and who is notified when significant changes occur.

The cadence is also not purely reactive. Even when no trigger events have fired, a quarterly calendar review verifies that the card accurately reflects the current production state. This combination of event-driven updates and calendar-driven reviews ensures that model cards remain reliable governance artifacts rather than one-time documents that drift from reality within months of publication.

attrs.alt: Model card maintenance cadence showing five trigger types (model version change, new evaluations, incident findings, tool/scope changes, calendar review) mapped to a six-step update workflow and version control strategy

content.text: Why Maintenance Cadence Matters

Stale model cards create liability. An enterprise customer who deployed an AI system based on a model card that described an accuracy rate of 94% has a legitimate grievance if the card was never updated to reflect a 78% accuracy rate on an important demographic after a model update. A regulatory auditor who finds that a model card does not reflect a known limitation that was identified in a red-team exercise three months ago will rate that as a significant finding.

More fundamentally, the maintenance cadence signals whether the organization actually reads and uses its model cards. A card that has not been updated in 18 months while the model has been retrained twice and through one production incident is evidence that the documentation is performative rather than operational. Governance programs that maintain living model cards demonstrate that documentation is a genuine tool for oversight, not a compliance decoration.
Benefits
Keeps model cards accurate as governance artifacts that auditors and enterprise customers can rely on.
Forces the organization to track model lifecycle events systematically — each update trigger is also a change management event.
Provides an audit trail of model evolution: version history shows when the model changed, what changed, and what the governance response was.
Alerts enterprise customers to material changes in the AI system they have deployed, supporting their own governance obligations.
Prevents the 'model card drift' pattern where the card describes the model as it was at launch, not as it currently operates.
Trade-offs
Each update requires technical review, legal review, and potentially customer notification — the process must be efficient enough that teams follow it without excessive burden.
Frequent minor updates create changelog noise that makes it harder to identify major changes; the versioning strategy must distinguish signal from noise.
Notification obligations on major updates require pre-established customer notification channels and may trigger contractual obligations to notify within defined windows.
Trigger Types and Response Windows
Model Version Change

Any model retraining, fine-tuning, or change to system prompts that materially affects behavior is a major or minor card update trigger, depending on the scope of change. A full retraining on new data is a major version update requiring full card review. A prompt change that adjusts tone without changing capabilities is a minor update. Either way, the card must be reviewed within 14 days of the change being promoted to production.

The challenge is detection: engineering teams must notify the card owner when model changes occur. This notification should be built into the CI/CD pipeline — when a model promotion event fires, the compliance tooling receives a notification that initiates the card review workflow. Relying on informal communication means changes will be missed.
New Evaluation Results

When a new benchmark run, red-team exercise, fairness evaluation, or human evaluation is completed, the results must be incorporated into the model card within a defined window (typically 14 days for favorable results, 5 days for adverse findings). New evaluation results that reveal previously undisclosed limitations are the highest priority updates: these are the findings that, if not incorporated, create liability.
Incident Findings

When a production incident reveals a model behavior that was not described in the card — a hallucination pattern, a bias finding from a complaint, an adversarial exploit — the card must be updated within five business days to document the finding and the response. This is the fastest-SLA update type because the finding is public (a customer experienced it) and the failure to document it in the card is itself a governance failure.
Tool and Scope Changes

When an agent's tool access changes — a new tool is added, an existing tool's permissions are modified, or a deployment scope changes (e.g., expanding to a new user population) — the system card and any affected model cards must be reviewed for accuracy. This trigger is often missed because tool changes are treated as purely engineering decisions rather than governance events.
Calendar Review (Quarterly Minimum)

Regardless of whether any trigger events have fired, a quarterly calendar review verifies that the card accurately reflects the current production state. The reviewer checks: is the model version described in the card the actual production version? Are the accuracy figures from the most recent evaluation? Are the tools listed complete? Are the limitations still the known set? Are the contact details current? Discrepancies from any of these checks initiate an update cycle.
Mental Model: The Living Medical Record

A model card is like a patient's medical record. A medical record created at the patient's first visit and never updated — no new diagnoses, no changes in medication, no surgical history — is not a medical record, it is a historical anecdote. Doctors treating the patient months later cannot rely on it. A well-maintained medical record captures each significant event in the patient's health history, enabling any clinician to understand the current state and make informed decisions. Model cards are the medical record of the AI system — they must capture each significant event in the system's lifecycle for any governance stakeholder (auditor, customer, regulator, incident responder) to understand the current state.
Implementation: Maintenance Tracking System

"""
model_card_maintenance.py
Tracks model card update obligations and manages the update workflow.
Integrates with CI/CD pipeline to detect model changes automatically.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional, Literal
import json

UpdateTrigger = Literal[
    "model_version_change", "new_evaluation", "incident_finding",
    "tool_change", "scope_change", "calendar_review"
]
VersionType = Literal["major", "minor", "patch"]
UpdateStatus = Literal["open", "in_review", "complete", "overdue"]

SLA_DAYS: dict[UpdateTrigger, int] = {
    "incident_finding": 5,
    "model_version_change": 14,
    "new_evaluation": 14,
    "tool_change": 14,
    "scope_change": 14,
    "calendar_review": 14,
}

VERSION_TYPE_BY_TRIGGER: dict[UpdateTrigger, VersionType] = {
    "model_version_change": "major",
    "new_evaluation": "minor",
    "incident_finding": "minor",
    "tool_change": "minor",
    "scope_change": "minor",
    "calendar_review": "patch",
}

@dataclass
class ModelCardUpdateTask:
    task_id: str
    model_id: str
    card_current_version: str
    trigger: UpdateTrigger
    trigger_description: str
    triggered_at: datetime
    assigned_to: str
    status: UpdateStatus = "open"
    sla_days: int = field(init=False)
    version_type: VersionType = field(init=False)
    completed_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    new_card_version: Optional[str] = None
    customer_notification_required: bool = False
    customer_notified_at: Optional[datetime] = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.sla_days = SLA_DAYS.get(self.trigger, 14)
        self.version_type = VERSION_TYPE_BY_TRIGGER.get(self.trigger, "minor")
        self.customer_notification_required = (
            self.version_type == "major"
        )

    @property
    def due_date(self) -> date:
        return (self.triggered_at + timedelta(days=self.sla_days)).date()

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return self.status == "open" and date.today() > self.due_date

    def complete(self, new_version: str) -> None:
        self.status = "complete"
        self.completed_at = datetime.utcnow()
        self.new_card_version = new_version

def increment_version(current: str, version_type: VersionType) -> str:
    """Increment semantic version string."""
    parts = [int(x) for x in current.split(".")]
    if version_type == "major":
        parts = [parts[0] + 1, 0, 0]
    elif version_type == "minor":
        parts = [parts[0], parts[1] + 1, 0]
    else:  # patch
        parts = [parts[0], parts[1], parts[2] + 1]
    return ".".join(str(p) for p in parts)

class ModelCardRegistry:
    def __init__(self):
        self.tasks: list[ModelCardUpdateTask] = []
        self._task_counter = 0

    def _next_id(self) -> str:
        self._task_counter += 1
        return f"MCU-{date.today().strftime('%Y%m%d')}-{self._task_counter:03d}"

    def create_update_task(
        self,
        model_id: str,
        current_version: str,
        trigger: UpdateTrigger,
        description: str,
        assigned_to: str,
    ) -> ModelCardUpdateTask:
        task = ModelCardUpdateTask(
            task_id=self._next_id(),
            model_id=model_id,
            card_current_version=current_version,
            trigger=trigger,
            trigger_description=description,
            triggered_at=datetime.utcnow(),
            assigned_to=assigned_to,
        )
        self.tasks.append(task)
        return task

    def overdue_tasks(self) -> list[ModelCardUpdateTask]:
        return [t for t in self.tasks if t.is_overdue()]

    def status_report(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "report_date": str(date.today()),
            "total_open_tasks": sum(1 for t in self.tasks if t.status == "open"),
            "overdue": len(self.overdue_tasks()),
            "pending_customer_notification": sum(
                1 for t in self.tasks
                if t.customer_notification_required and t.customer_notified_at is None
                and t.status == "complete"
            ),
            "tasks": [
                {
                    "task_id": t.task_id,
                    "model_id": t.model_id,
                    "trigger": t.trigger,
                    "due_date": str(t.due_date),
                    "status": t.status,
                    "version_type": t.version_type,
                }
                for t in self.tasks
            ],
        }

# Example usage
registry = ModelCardRegistry()

# Trigger from CI/CD pipeline: model retrained
task1 = registry.create_update_task(
    model_id="credit-underwriting-agent",
    current_version="2.0.4",
    trigger="model_version_change",
    description="Model retrained with Q2 2026 data; base model upgraded to claude-sonnet-4-6",
    assigned_to="alice@company.com",
)
print(f"Created task {task1.task_id}, due {task1.due_date}, version type: {task1.version_type}")
print(f"New version will be: {increment_version(task1.card_current_version, task1.version_type)}")
print(f"Customer notification required: {task1.customer_notification_required}")

# Trigger from incident response
task2 = registry.create_update_task(
    model_id="customer-support-chatbot",
    current_version="3.1.2",
    trigger="incident_finding",
    description="INC-2026-0042: session isolation bug revealed context leak; add to known limitations",
    assigned_to="bob@company.com",
)
print(f"\nCreated task {task2.task_id}, due {task2.due_date} (5-day SLA)")

print("\nStatus report:")
print(json.dumps(registry.status_report(), indent=2))
Version Control for Model Cards

# CHANGELOG.md for model card version history
# Maintains an auditable record of all card updates.

model: credit-underwriting-agent
card_repository: git://docs.internal/model-cards/credit-underwriting-agent

changelog:
  - version: "3.0.0"
    date: 2026-07-19
    update_type: major
    trigger: model_version_change
    triggered_by: CI/CD pipeline (model-promotion-event)
    updated_by: alice@company.com
    reviewed_by: [ai-lead@company.com, compliance@company.com]
    changes:
      - "Updated base model from claude-haiku to claude-sonnet-4-6"
      - "Updated accuracy benchmarks from Q1 evaluation (91.2% → 93.1% on primary task)"
      - "Added new fairness evaluation section with Q2 2026 demographic parity results"
      - "Updated training data section: Q2 2026 data incorporated"
    customer_notification: true
    notification_date: 2026-07-21
    notification_channel: vendor trust portal + email to enterprise customers

  - version: "2.1.0"
    date: 2026-05-10
    update_type: minor
    trigger: incident_finding
    triggered_by: INC-2026-0031
    updated_by: alice@company.com
    reviewed_by: [legal@company.com, ciso@company.com]
    changes:
      - "Added to known limitations: model accuracy degrades for applicants with income from multiple sources"
      - "Added mitigation note: human review mandatory for multi-source income applications"
    customer_notification: false
    notes: "Internal incident; limitation added proactively; no customer notification required"

  - version: "2.0.4"
    date: 2026-04-01
    update_type: patch
    trigger: calendar_review
    triggered_by: Q1 quarterly review
    updated_by: alice@company.com
    reviewed_by: [ai-lead@company.com]
    changes:
      - "Updated team contact email (personnel change)"
      - "Corrected typo in evaluation methodology section"
    customer_notification: false
Real-World Example: Credit Scoring Model Card Drift

A fintech company's credit scoring model card described accuracy of 91% at the time of initial deployment. Over 18 months, the model was retrained three times, a red-team exercise found a bias pattern for applicants with non-traditional employment histories, and a customer complaint revealed an edge case with self-employment income that the model handled inconsistently. None of these events triggered a card update because there was no defined maintenance cadence — updates were supposed to happen 'when the team has time.' When an enterprise banking customer reviewed the model card during their due diligence for a partnership, they discovered that the card described a model version that was 18 months and three retraining cycles out of date, and contained no mention of the known bias finding. They terminated the partnership discussion.

After this incident, the company implemented a maintenance cadence with explicit trigger types, update SLAs, and a quarterly calendar review. The model card was rebuilt to describe the current state, including the known limitations. The subsequent enterprise customer onboarding cycle completed successfully — the new card's honest treatment of limitations actually increased customer confidence by demonstrating that the team actively monitored and documented model behavior.
Common Mistakes
Treating model card updates as an optional 'when we have time' activity rather than a time-boxed obligation with SLAs.
Not building trigger detection into the CI/CD pipeline — relying on informal communication means version changes are regularly missed.
Updating the card version without updating the card content — a 'v3.0' label on unchanged v1 content is misleading.
Not notifying enterprise customers of major model card changes that affect the system they have deployed.
Allowing the 'last reviewed' date on the card to become outdated without a true review having occurred — this creates false impression of currency.
Best Practices
Integrate model card update triggers directly into CI/CD pipeline events — automate the notification to the card owner when a model promotion event fires.
Use semantic versioning (major.minor.patch) consistently so that version numbers communicate the magnitude of change without requiring readers to compare versions.
Store model cards in the same version control repository as the model artifacts so that git history provides an automatic audit trail of card changes.
For enterprise customers, provide a stable URL for the current model card and a separate URL for the changelog so they can monitor for changes without periodically re-reading the full card.
Conduct the quarterly calendar review with a checklist of specific facts to verify (production version, accuracy metrics, tool list, contact info) rather than a general read-through.
Require two reviewers for all model card updates: the AI technical owner (for factual accuracy) and a compliance or legal reviewer (for liability implications of the language).
Performance and Scalability

Organizations with large model portfolios (10+ deployed models) should automate the maintenance cadence where possible: automated detection of version change events from the model registry; automated calendar review reminders; and an integrated dashboard showing card currency status (current version deployed vs. card version, days since last review, open update tasks). GRC platforms and model registries like MLflow or SageMaker Model Registry can be integrated to provide these capabilities.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability

Model card version history is governance-critical evidence. The version control system storing model cards must have access controls that prevent modification of historical versions — committed card versions should be immutable once merged to the main branch. Use branch protection rules and require signed commits for model card changes so that the audit trail of who changed what when is cryptographically verifiable.
Key Takeaways
A model card published at deployment and never updated is a historical document, not a governance artifact — the maintenance cadence is what makes it a living record.
Five trigger types drive updates: model version change (14-day SLA), new evaluations (14 days), incident findings (5 days), tool/scope changes (14 days), and quarterly calendar review.
Incident findings have the tightest update SLA (5 business days) because the limitation is already public — every day the card does not reflect it is an additional governance failure.
Use semantic versioning to communicate change magnitude: major for full retraining, minor for new evaluations or limitation additions, patch for factual corrections.
Build trigger detection into the CI/CD pipeline — manual communication of model changes will be missed; automation is the only reliable mechanism at scale.
Enterprise customers must be notified of major version updates (full model retraining) so they can re-evaluate their own governance positions.
Two-reviewer approval (technical + compliance) is the minimum governance gate for all model card updates — single-reviewer updates risk factual errors or liability-creating language.
